topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found the manuscript tucked beneath a stack of forgotten correspondence—a sprawling, intimate chronicle of my life, narrated with an unnerving level of detail. My hands, usually steady, trembled as I traced the elegant script detailing my most private anxieties and fleeting joys. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach and spread outward, tightening my chest until breathing became a shallow, ragged thing. I didn't scream. Instead, I carefully closed the file, the sound echoing loudly in the silence, and walked out, the click of the door latch sounding like a final judgment. Every subsequent action was precise, calculated, focused entirely on dismantling the edifice of their creation, piece by agonizing piece.

[story 2]

"You've been watching me," I accused, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. Liam looked up, his expression shifting from mild surprise to something guarded. It was the realization that sent a sharp, unwelcome jolt through me—the undeniable proof that he had been meticulously documenting every shared glance, every whispered secret, transforming our reality into his literary subject. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. I didn't raise my voice; that would have been too easy. I just stared, letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable, a vacuum ready to implode. When he finally tried to offer an explanation, his tone was too light, too breezy. I simply turned away, the movement stiff, deliberately refusing to acknowledge his presence, letting the sheer weight of my displeasure settle over him like a physical shroud.

[story 3]

The first time I saw the dedication page, a strange, hollow emptiness settled in my core. It wasn't anger that flared; it was a deep, chilling resolve. He had taken the raw material of my existence and polished it into something public, something permanent. A slow, deliberate tightening began in my shoulders, a hardening of the muscles beneath my skin. I spent the next hour methodically cataloging every shared memory mentioned in the text, cross-referencing dates and locations with my own recollection, searching for the seams, the inconsistencies he might have overlooked. When I finally confronted him, my voice was dangerously low, stripped bare of warmth. "You thought this was just a hobby?" I asked, my eyes fixed on his, reflecting nothing but cold, unwavering intent. The air between us grew thick, charged with a tension that promised a reckoning far worse than mere argument.
